articles.title: Scaling Managed Agents: Decoupling the brain from the hands
articles.description: Harnesses encode assumptions that go stale as models improve. Managed Agents—our hosted service for long-horizon agent work—is built around interfaces that stay stable as harnesses change.
articles.summary: Harnesses encode assumptions that go stale as models improve. Managed Agents—our hosted service for long-horizon agent work—is built around interfaces that stay stable as harnesses change.
guidance: Building Managed Agents meant solving an old problem in computing: how to design a system for “ programs as yet unthought of .” Decades ago, operating systems solved this problem by virtualizing hardware into abstractions— process, file —general enough for programs that didn't exist yet.
Managed Agents follow the same pattern.
A running topic on the Engineering Blog is how to build effective agents and design harnesses for long-running work .

articles.title: Harness design for long-running application development
articles.description: Harness design is key to performance at the frontier of agentic coding. Here's how we pushed Claude further in frontend design and long-running autonomous software engineering.
articles.summary: Harness design is key to performance at the frontier of agentic coding. Here's how we pushed Claude further in frontend design and long-running autonomous software engineering.
guidance: We've previously shown that harness design has a substantial impact on the effectiveness of long running agentic coding.
Two insights shaped the harness I built for frontend design.

Design quality: Does the design feel like a coherent whole rather than a collection of parts? Strong work here means the colors, typography, layout, imagery, and other details combine to create a distinct mood and identity.
For more complex tasks, the agent still tends to go off the rails over time.
I started by experimenting on frontend design, where the self-evaluation issue was most visible.
headings: Frontend design: making subjective quality gradable

articles.title: Quantifying infrastructure noise in agentic coding evals
articles.description: Infrastructure configuration can swing agentic coding benchmarks by several percentage points—sometimes more than the leaderboard gap between top models.
articles.summary: Infrastructure configuration can swing agentic coding benchmarks by several percentage points—sometimes more than the leaderboard gap between top models.
guidance: Agentic coding evals are different: models are given a full environment where they write programs, run tests, install dependencies, and iterate over multiple turns.

Agentic coding benchmarks like SWE-bench and Terminal-Bench are commonly used to compare the software engineering capabilities of frontier models—with top spots on leaderboards often separated by just a few percentage points.
Eval developers have begun accounting for this.
Terminal-Bench 2.0, for instance, specifies recommended CPU and RAM on a per-task basis in their latest 2.0 release.
We also tested whether this pattern holds on evals outside Terminal-Bench by running a crossover experiment on SWE-bench.
This finding raised a larger question: how much does resource configuration impact evaluation scores?

articles.title: Designing AI resistant technical evaluations
articles.description: What we learned from three iterations of a performance engineering take-home that Claude keeps beating.
articles.summary: What we learned from three iterations of a performance engineering take-home that Claude keeps beating.
guidance: Evaluating technical candidates becomes harder as AI capabilities improve.

I've now iterated through three versions of our take-home in an attempt to ensure it still carries signal. Each time, I’ve learned something new about what makes evaluations robust to AI assistance and what doesn't.
Each time, I’ve learned something new about what makes evaluations robust to AI assistance and what doesn't.
A take-home that distinguishes well between human skill levels today may be trivially solved by models tomorrow—rendering it useless for evaluation.
Tristan designed—and redesigned—the take-home test that's helped Anthropic hire dozens of performance engineers.

We needed a way to evaluate candidates more efficiently. So, I took two weeks to design a take-home test that could adequately capture the demands of the role and identify the most capable applicants.
